definition: Represent patients by baseline or longitudinal covariates and compare them with a clinically motivated or learned metric for time-to-event prediction, such as weighted nearest-neighbor kernels or Mahalanobis-like distances. For SPECTRA, this audits whether survival-model performance depends on evaluation patients having close analogs in the training cohort under the metric used for survival prediction.
example_usage: python tabular_mahalanobis_similarity.py --train train_patients.csv --eval eval_patients.csv --out pairwise_similarity.csv --feature-cols-prefix cov_

citations.title: Distance-Metric Learning for Personalized Survival Analysis

evidence.summary: The paper proposes weighted nearest neighbors for personalized survival prediction and learns a distance metric.
It formalizes patient similarity with kernel weights derived from a learned metric in covariate space.
The paper emphasizes nearest-neighbor interpretability for survival analysis.

limitations: Learning the similarity metric from survival outcomes can leak label information into an audit axis if not done inside training folds.
Censoring, competing risks, and non-proportional hazards can make one patient metric misleading.
A metric optimized for prediction may not be the same as a clinically meaningful novelty axis.
quality_gates: State whether the metric was fixed a priori, learned only on training data, or learned retrospectively.
Report censoring distribution, time horizon, covariates, scaling, and missingness handling.
Compare to simple clinical-tabular and temporal/site similarity axes.
